Justification: Foundational security control ensuring core directives injected at session start. Non-negotiable for policy enforcement across all stages. DORA Art. 9(4) requires documented ICT security policies.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Blocks all downstream controls if missing

Justification: Required for audit trail and incident reporting compliance. Mandatory for DORA Art. 17 (ICT incident management) and EU AI Act Art. 12 (record-keeping). Non-negotiable for regulatory proof.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Missing audit trail violates regulatory requirements

Justification: Foundational traceability across entire SDLC. Required by DORA Art. 8(1) (document all ICT-supported functions and assets) and EU AI Act Art. 12 (logging of AI system usage).
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Impossible to prove end-to-end compliance without traceability

Justification: Required for third-party oversight (DORA Art. 28) and deployer obligations (EU AI Act Art. 26(5)). Essential for supply chain security and monitoring AI systems.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Missing third-party/agent oversight creates compliance gap

Justification: Establishes source of truth for all agentic actions. Prevents propagation of invalid requirements downstream. Foundational for quality and compliance.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Invalid specs cascade into all subsequent stages

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 8(2) to continuously identify ICT risk sources and assess cyber threats. Foundational for AI risk management (EU AI Act Art. 9). Cannot proceed without risk tier.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Risk management cannot proceed without classification

Justification: Required by EU AI Act Art. 6 (classification rules for high-risk AI) and Art. 9 (risk management system). Determines entire compliance regime. Non-negotiable for AI systems.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Determines which AI Act requirements apply

Justification: Establishes starting point for end-to-end traceability required by DORA Art. 8(1) and EU AI Act Art. 12. Source of truth for audit trail.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Root traceability point; missing here breaks downstream audit trail

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 9 (ICT security policies) and EU AI Act Art. 15(4) (resilience against manipulation). Prevents malicious intent from entering development cycle.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Prevents prohibited/harmful systems from starting development

Justification: High-value quality control preventing conflicting requirements upstream. Not blocking but significantly improves development efficiency and reduces rework.
Remediation Impact: MEDIUM - Conflicting specs cause mid-cycle rework

Justification: Ensures design meets standards before coding begins. Critical gate to prevent costly rework. Required for quality assurance.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Incomplete designs cascade to implementation rework

Justification: Human-required control ensuring design risk is formally assessed before coding. Critical governance gate blocking code implementation.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Blocks deployment of risky designs

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 25 (testing methodologies) and EU AI Act Art. 9 (risk management). Threat model is foundation for security testing and mitigation.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Security testing roadmap depends on threat model

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 9(4) and EU AI Act Art. 14 (human oversight). Injects stage-specific directives governing agent behavior. Non-negotiable for human oversight and policy enforcement.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Missing stage directives break oversight chain

Justification: Required by EU AI Act Art. 10 (data governance), Art. 13 (transparency), and Art. 14 (human oversight). Critical gate for AI systems to address data quality, transparency, and oversight.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Missing AI design review breaches multiple AI Act articles

Justification: Important for code quality and peer review but can be supplemented with automated checks. Supports DORA Art. 8(5) (change management) but not blocking.
Remediation Impact: MEDIUM - Code changes tracked but not strictly gated

Justification: Automated enforcement of code quality. Improves maintainability and reduces technical debt. Not blocking deployment but significant for long-term maintainability.
Remediation Impact: MEDIUM - Technical debt accumulates without code quality standards

Justification: Supports audit trail and compliance reporting (GC-0A). Important for demonstrating decision rationale but not blocking deployment.
Remediation Impact: MEDIUM - Audit trail incomplete without decision log

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 9(4) (access controls and authentication). Prevents unauthorized code changes and enforces least privilege.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Missing access controls violate DORA Art. 9 requirements

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 9 and EU AI Act Art. 15(4) (resilience against manipulation). Detects and prevents malicious code or policy violations in final code.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Post-guardrails prevent malicious code from reaching testing

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 28 (supply chain security) and EU AI Act Art. 15(3) (supply chain integrity). Prevents exposure of sensitive credentials.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Exposed credentials cause immediate compliance breach

Justification: Required by EU AI Act Art. 17(1)(f) (data/documentation requirements) and DORA Art. 28 (third-party oversight). Essential for supply chain security and AI audit trail.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Cannot prove code provenance for regulatory audit

Justification: Critical quality gate but automated testing tooling can substitute some manual testing. Required for verification but gates compliance more through exit criteria.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Missing functional testing prevents verification gate

Justification: Important for AI systems but only applicable to AI components. Supports EU AI Act Art. 15 (accuracy/robustness) but not blocking if no AI present.
Remediation Impact: HIGH (for AI) - Missing AI output validation breaches Art. 15

Justification: Required by EU AI Act Art. 11 (technical documentation) and Annex IV. Non-negotiable for regulatory proof of AI system compliance.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Missing documentation violates EU AI Act Art. 11

Justification: Required by EU AI Act Art. 9(7) (testing to verify risk management measures). Blocks deployment if residual risk exceeds tolerance. Critical gate.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Blocks high-risk deployment; gates stage exit

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 24-25 (digital operational resilience testing). Static analysis is foundational security testing for identifying code-level vulnerabilities.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - SAST is baseline security testing requirement

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 24-25 (operational resilience testing including penetration testing). Dynamic analysis is foundational for runtime security validation.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - DAST is baseline security testing requirement

Justification: Required by EU AI Act Art. 10 (data governance for bias), Art. 15 (robustness). Critical for high-risk AI but only applicable to AI components.
Remediation Impact: HIGH (for AI) - Missing bias testing breaches Art. 10/15

Justification: Comprehensive gate checklist supporting change management (DORA Art. 8(5)). Important but gates through exit criteria.
Remediation Impact: MEDIUM - Incomplete readiness checklist gates deployment

Justification: Required by change management governance (DORA Art. 8(5)). Human-required control ensuring deployment decision is formally authorized.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Blocks unauthorized deployments

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 11 (ICT business continuity). Important for recovery but gates exit through rollback readiness.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Missing rollback plan blocks recovery capability

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 24-25 (testing of infrastructure). Ensures production environment meets security requirements before deployment.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Infrastructure vulnerabilities expose production systems

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 28 (supply chain security) and EU AI Act Art. 15(3) (supply chain integrity). Ensures code/model integrity during deployment.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Deployment tampering bypasses all prior controls

Justification: Efficiency optimization for operational monitoring. Nice-to-have for SLA tracking but not blocking. Supports Phase 3 optimization.
Remediation Impact: LOW - Absence affects operational efficiency not compliance

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 10-11 (detection and monitoring) for continuous risk assessment. Important for ongoing compliance but monitoring rather than blocking.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Missing risk monitoring breaks continuous compliance

Justification: Required by DORA Art. 10 (detection mechanisms) and Art. 17-19 (incident management and reporting). Critical for incident identification and regulatory reporting.
Remediation Impact: CRITICAL - Missing incident detection blocks regulatory reporting

Justification: Advanced monitoring for operational security. Complements SC-6A with enhanced detection but not blocking. Phase 3 optimization.
Remediation Impact: LOW - Enhancement to SC-6A; not blocking if SC-6A present

Justification: Required by EU AI Act Art. 72 (post-market monitoring) and Art. 26(5) (deployer monitoring obligations). Critical for deployed AI systems but monitoring rather than blocking.
Remediation Impact: HIGH - Missing AI surveillance violates Art. 72
